premise: VeloceTech S.p.A., a Milan-based software company, launched a gender-based violence prevention programme in 2023 after a survey revealed 28% of female staff had experienced harassment or threatening behaviour. The company partnered with a certified support organisation to deliver quarterly awareness workshops for all 340 employees, appointed a dedicated welfare officer trained in GBV case management, and established a confidential reporting channel. It contributed €80,000 to local shelter services and publicly reported prevention expenditure. Six formal complaints were received and resolved in the channel's first year. By year-end, 91% of employees confirmed awareness of available support pathways, and zero cases escalated to external authorities unchallenged. VeloceTech's programme was cited by the Lombardy Regional Authority as a model for private sector GBV response.

Rheinkapital AG, a Frankfurt-based asset management firm, integrated a gender mainstreaming methodology into all five of its EU-co-funded investment programmes in 2022. Each programme underwent a gender impact assessment prior to disbursement conducted by an external equality specialist. The firm appointed gender equality coordinators in each of its three investment divisions and introduced mandatory gender-disaggregated reporting for all portfolio companies receiving EU structural funds. Procurement contracts above €50,000 were subject to a gender-responsive clause requiring suppliers to demonstrate active equal-opportunity policies. By the end of the financial year, 100% of EU-funded programmes had been reviewed and adjusted under the framework, and gender-disaggregated outcome data was published in Rheinkapital's annual sustainability report.

Clínica SaludMed S.A., a private hospital network across Barcelona and Valencia, Spain, conducted a comprehensive pay equity audit in 2023 covering 620 employees across twelve job families. The independent audit revealed an unexplained gender pay gap of 14.3% concentrated in nursing management and administrative coordination roles. The hospital revised its salary bands, reclassified six job titles following a gender-neutral evaluation process, and issued pay corrections to 87 employees, 79 of whom were women. Following remediation, the adjusted gender pay gap fell to 2.1%. Clínica SaludMed published the full audit findings and remediation steps in its 2023 annual report, becoming the first private healthcare group in Catalonia to do so voluntarily.

The Małopolska Regional Education Authority in Kraków, Poland, introduced a mandatory pay transparency register for all 214 public schools under its jurisdiction in September 2022. Each school was required to publish anonymised salary ranges by role and seniority on its institutional website. The Authority established a dedicated equal pay complaint unit staffed by two labour law specialists, which processed 31 complaints in its first year and issued binding corrective orders in 18 cases. Training on the equal pay principle was delivered to all 214 head teachers and school administrators. By December 2023, the average gender pay gap across the network had narrowed from 11.8% to 6.4%, verified by an independent audit commissioned by the Authority.

Cork City Council, Ireland, overhauled its grading system in 2022 by replacing legacy seniority-based classification with a gender-neutral job evaluation framework developed with the Irish Human Rights and Equality Commission. The new framework evaluated 48 job families across six factors — skills, effort, responsibility, working conditions, decision-making authority, and impact — without reference to gendered occupational hierarchies. Following regrading, 23 roles were elevated, benefiting 67 employees, 58 of whom were women. The Council set a target of 45% female representation in senior management and reached 41% by December 2023, up from 27% in 2020. The methodology was published as an open-source toolkit for other local authorities.

Jämlikhetsfonden, a Stockholm-based equality foundation, launched a biannual Gender Pay Tracker in 2022 in collaboration with Statistics Sweden, publishing sector-level gender pay gap data disaggregated by age, occupation, contract type, and part-time incidence, covering approximately 2.3 million workers. A dedicated pension gap module calculated the cumulative impact of career breaks and part-time work on women's retirement incomes across five pension cohorts. The first edition documented an average adjusted pay gap of 7.2% and an overall earnings gap of 28.4%. The Tracker was downloaded 14,000 times in its first six months and cited in three parliamentary inquiries into pay discrimination reform. The second edition was published on schedule in late 2023.

NordStaal B.V., a Rotterdam-based steel manufacturer, pre-emptively adopted a company-level gender equality strategy in 2021 modelled on the forthcoming EU framework. The strategy included four pillars: equal pay certification, women-in-STEM recruitment targets, gender-sensitive ergonomics in production facilities, and eight-week paid paternity leave. An independent pay equity review identified a 9.7% raw gender pay gap, reduced to 3.2% after controlling for role and seniority. NordStaal set a binding target of closing the residual gap to below 1% by 2024 and submitted its strategy to the Dutch Social and Economic Council as a sectoral model. By 2023, female employment in technical roles had grown from 8% to 14%.

MediaPro România S.A., a Bucharest-based broadcasting conglomerate, adopted a formal Gender Equality in Leadership Policy in March 2022. The policy established mandatory gender balance targets for all editorial boards, production committees, and senior editorial teams, setting a minimum representation floor of 40% for either sex. A cross-departmental steering committee reviewed appointment decisions quarterly. By December 2023, women held 44% of editorial board seats and 38% of programme director positions, up from 29% and 22% respectively in 2020. The company also launched a mentorship programme pairing 30 junior female journalists with senior editors, with 12 participants promoted within 18 months of completing the programme.

Lusitano Resorts S.A., a hotel and hospitality group operating across Lisbon and the Algarve, Portugal, launched a Women in Work initiative in 2021 targeting the persistent gender employment gap in its sector. The group recruited 210 women into full-time positions previously dominated by men, including maintenance, kitchen management, and security. It introduced flexible shift scheduling and subsidised childcare at two flagship resorts, increasing female workforce participation from 41% to 54% by 2023. The gender employment gap within the group fell from 18 percentage points in 2019 to 8 percentage points by 2023, placing the group ahead of the national 2030 trajectory towards halving the gap.

HermesCargo S.A., a logistics company based in Piraeus, Greece, overhauled its parental leave policy in 2022 following the transposition of the EU Work-Life Balance Directive. The company extended paid paternity leave from 2 to 10 days and introduced a fully paid 16-week parental leave scheme available to both mothers and fathers on equal terms. In 2023, 47 fathers took parental leave, a 340% increase from the previous year. The share of caring responsibilities reported by male employees in the company's wellbeing survey rose from 18% to 41%. Female attrition among employees with children under five fell by 31% compared to 2021, confirming the positive retention effect of the new policy.

InnovaTech France S.A., a Paris-listed software services company, introduced a structured Women in Leadership programme in 2022 after an internal review found that women constituted only 12% of senior management and 9% of board members. The programme included sponsorship pairings between executive directors and high-potential female managers, unconscious bias training for 180 hiring managers, and a revised promotion scorecard incorporating gender diversity indicators. A €500,000 Women in Engineering scholarship fund was also established. By December 2023, female representation in senior management reached 22% and board membership rose to 19%. The company set a 2026 target of 40% female board representation and published a quarterly progress dashboard on its investor relations website.

BelgaFinance NV, a Brussels-listed investment bank, adopted a comprehensive Gender Equality Policy in January 2022 covering the full employee lifecycle. The policy mandated gender-balanced shortlists for all roles at grade seven and above, required each female associate to be assigned a senior sponsor, and established a structured career development framework with gender-specific guidance modules. The bank partnered with Ghent University to deliver a tailored leadership programme for 40 mid-level female managers annually. In 2023, 62% of external hires at senior levels came from gender-balanced shortlists, and 18 of the 40 programme participants received promotions within 12 months. Female representation at managing director level rose from 16% to 24%.

FarmaGroup S.p.A., a Milan-listed pharmaceutical manufacturer, launched a board renewal programme in 2022 to comply with the Women on Boards Directive. Following a rigorous external search process conducted by a gender-aware executive search firm, three new female non-executive directors were appointed between March 2022 and June 2023. The appointments raised female non-executive board membership from 25% to 44%, exceeding the 40% threshold two years ahead of the June 2026 statutory deadline. The nomination committee revised its candidate assessment criteria to include explicit references to diversity objectives. FarmaGroup published its board gender composition data and appointment methodology in its 2023 annual corporate governance report.

Lernwelt AG, a Düsseldorf-listed educational technology company, revised its director appointment process in 2022 to embed gender-balance criteria at every stage, from role specification through to final board presentation. The nomination committee adopted a requirement that all shortlists for non-executive positions include at least one candidate of each sex. Candidate evaluations were conducted using a structured scoring rubric separating objective competency criteria from cultural-fit assessments to reduce bias. External search firms were contractually required to demonstrate gender-balanced long lists. In the two appointment rounds conducted in 2023, all four shortlists met the gender balance requirement, and two of the three positions filled were awarded to women. Female non-executive representation rose from 27% to 38%.

Energía Ibérica S.A., a Madrid-listed energy utility, began publishing a dedicated Gender Balance in Governance report annually from 2022. The report disclosed the gender composition of the board and each of its committees, appointments in the preceding year disaggregated by sex, candidate selection criteria, and a roadmap to reach the 40% non-executive target by 2026. The 2023 edition documented that women held 35% of non-executive positions, up from 23% in 2020, and set out three concrete recruitment measures for the following year. The report was reviewed and verified by an external auditor and included in the company's annual integrated report filed with Spain's National Securities Market Commission.

FundacjaPol, a Warsaw-based governance advisory foundation, worked with 12 Polish listed companies in 2022 to implement board diversity action plans compliant with the Women on Boards Directive. Each company signed a legally binding memorandum committing to specific gender representation targets and annual reporting obligations. FundacjaPol provided each company with a tailored nomination process review and access to a database of 240 pre-screened qualified female board candidates. Across the 12 companies, female board representation increased from an average of 18% to 31% over 24 months. Three companies reached the 40% non-executive threshold by December 2023. FundacjaPol published a progress report confirming all 12 companies maintained or increased their female board share year-on-year.

CelticSteel plc, a Dublin-listed precision engineering manufacturer, submitted its board diversity compliance plan to the Irish Corporate Governance Authority in March 2023, committing to 40% female non-executive representation by 30 June 2026 under path (a) of the Directive. Two new female non-executive directors were appointed in 2023, raising female non-executive representation from 17% to 33%. An executive pipeline target of 35% female representation at director-minus-one level was set to sustain board candidate supply. A board effectiveness sub-committee was established to oversee diversity targets and report quarterly to the full board. By March 2024, female executive representation at that pipeline level stood at 32%, ahead of schedule.

NordMedia AB, a Stockholm-listed digital publishing company, submitted documentation to Sweden's Companies Registration Office in December 2022 demonstrating that women held 34% of its non-executive director positions as of 27 December 2022, thereby meeting the 30% threshold required for suspension eligibility under the Women on Boards Directive. The submission included a verified board composition registry, a gender balance governance report, and an external auditor's declaration confirming accuracy. NordMedia also provided evidence of an internal nominee pipeline showing 38% female candidates for future board appointments and committed to maintaining representation above 30% through a voluntary annual review. The Registration Office accepted the suspension documentation in February 2023.

organisation: Dutch Authority for the Financial Markets
premise: The Dutch Authority for the Financial Markets completed its first comprehensive Women on Boards Directive implementation report in November 2025 and submitted it to the European Commission on 18 December 2025, within the statutory deadline. The report covered all 147 listed companies subject to the Directive, including data on board gender composition, compliance status with each objective, and disaggregated information on non-executive versus executive representation. It reported that 63 companies, representing 43% of the total, had reached the 40% non-executive target. The Authority committed to publishing the next biennial report by December 2027, in accordance with the mandated reporting cycle.

LogiCar România S.A., a Bucharest-listed freight logistics company, submitted a detailed written contribution to the European Commission's 2030 review of the Women on Boards Directive in October 2030. The submission documented the company's progress from 9% to 43% female non-executive board representation over eight years and argued in favour of extending the Directive's scope to large non-listed companies. It presented sector data showing that Romanian logistics companies outside the Directive's scope showed virtually no progress, with average female board representation stagnating at 12%. LogiCar's contribution was cited in the Commission's official review report as an example of effective voluntary adoption beyond statutory requirements.

TechPortugal Cooperação S.A., a Lisbon-based international development firm, implemented a GAP III-aligned external action programme in Mozambique, Cape Verde, and São Tomé and Príncipe between 2021 and 2023. Each country programme integrated gender mainstreaming across all technical assistance activities, dedicated at least 25% of total budget to targeted gender equality actions, and included structured political dialogue sessions with national ministries on gender policy reform. By 2023, the firm had trained 420 local gender focal points, co-drafted two national gender equality strategies with partner governments, and achieved a 15 percentage point increase in women's participation in targeted digital economy sectors across the three countries.

Hellenic Research Finance S.A., an Athens-based research funding intermediary, administered a Horizon Europe-funded gender and innovation programme from 2022 to 2024, disbursing €4.2 million to 18 gender studies research consortia across Greek universities. A mandatory requirement specified that at least 50% of principal investigators were women. A gender integration checklist was applied to all funded proposals to ensure research design incorporated a gender perspective. By programme close, 11 of the 18 projects had published findings incorporating sex-disaggregated data, and three contributed to national policy revisions. The share of female principal investigators across the intermediary's full Horizon Europe portfolio rose from 31% to 47%.

SantéFrance International, a Paris-based public health NGO, developed country-level gender implementation plans for EU-funded programmes in Senegal and Mali in 2022, in accordance with GAP III requirements. Each plan was drafted through a participatory process involving six local women's rights organisations per country, whose representatives validated priorities and co-signed the final document. The Senegal plan identified sexual and reproductive health, FGM prevention, and women's access to primary healthcare as the three priority areas. By 2023, the Senegal programme had reached 38,000 women with targeted health interventions, and women's organisations had been contracted to deliver 60% of community outreach activities.

EduGlobal Belgium asbl, a Brussels-based international education policy organisation, served as the EU's civil society partner in three UN Commission on the Status of Women sessions between 2022 and 2024. In each session, EduGlobal co-drafted and circulated model gender-responsive language amendments for 12 draft resolutions on education, poverty, and climate, working in coordination with the EU delegation. Of the 12 resolutions, 9 were adopted with gender-responsive language intact or strengthened, including explicit references to girls' equal right to education in post-conflict settings. EduGlobal compiled a language guide published in four languages and disseminated to 60 national delegations.

The Municipality of Padova, Italy, funded a three-year programme targeting the elimination of female genital mutilation and early child marriage in Ethiopia and Somalia, launched in 2021 with a municipal contribution of €320,000 complemented by EU co-funding. The programme reached 14 communities and trained 180 community health workers, teachers, and religious leaders as gender equality advocates. By 2023, rates of FGM among girls under 15 in the 14 target communities had fallen by 38% according to community health records, and 60 families had signed voluntary pledges to abandon the practice. The Municipality published an annual impact report and presented findings at the 2023 European Municipal Gender Equality Forum in Vienna.

Humanitas Deutschland e.V., a Berlin-based humanitarian NGO, integrated a gender-based violence prevention and response protocol into all emergency operations in 2022. In refugee response operations in Jordan and Lebanon, the protocol mandated deployment of trained GBV case workers within 72 hours of emergency onset, establishment of safe spaces for women and girls within the first week, and provision of the minimum initial service package covering psychosocial support, clinical management of rape, and legal aid. In 2023, Humanitas served 6,800 GBV survivors across both operations, with 92% of cases referred to appropriate follow-up services. The protocol was subsequently adopted as a standard operating procedure across all Humanitas emergency programmes globally.

IndustriaGlobal España S.A., a Valencia-based manufacturing multinational, implemented a supplier code of conduct in 2022 requiring all partner factories to adopt zero-tolerance policies on gender-based violence and cooperate with local law enforcement. Working with a Moroccan legal reform NGO, IndustriaGlobal co-funded a capacity-building programme for 120 police officers in three production provinces on victim-centred investigation techniques and referral procedures. A baseline assessment showed only 23% of workers knew how to report GBV incidents; by programme end the figure had risen to 67%. IndustriaGlobal published a supplier compliance report confirming 94% of its 48 partner factories met the updated code of conduct requirements.

MediaAid Polska sp. z o.o., a Warsaw-based international media development organisation, managed an EU-funded health communication programme in Kenya and Uganda between 2021 and 2023, with a budget of €2.8 million. The programme produced and broadcast radio and television content on the harms of FGM and child marriage, reaching an estimated audience of 1.4 million. A companion mobile health platform provided sexual and reproductive health information to 28,000 registered users, of whom 71% were women and girls. Surveys at programme close showed a 22 percentage point increase in intention to abandon FGM among surveyed parents and a 19% increase in girls' access to modern contraception information.

Emerald Hospitality Group Ltd, a Dublin-based hotel and catering company, launched a Women's Financial Inclusion Initiative in 2022 through its international development arm, in partnership with Microfinance Ireland and a Kenyan women's cooperative bank. The initiative provided legal and financial literacy training to 800 women entrepreneurs in rural Kenya whose access to land titles and bank credit was restricted by customary inheritance practices. Working with local legal aid clinics, the initiative supported 140 women in formally registering land titles previously held informally and facilitated micro-credit access for 210 women previously ineligible for bank loans. Average annual income among participating women increased by 34% and business investment grew by 28% compared to a matched control group.

NordLogistik AB, a Gothenburg-based logistics company, channelled SEK 1.8 million of its corporate social responsibility budget through Sweden's development cooperation framework to a universal health coverage programme in Uganda in 2022. The programme, implemented by a local health NGO, focused on ensuring access to sexual and reproductive health services for women and girls with disabilities in three rural districts. Mobile health units with accessible examination facilities visited 45 communities, serving 4,200 women and girls. Disability-inclusive training was delivered to 90 community health workers. By 2023, access to assisted delivery among women with mobility impairments in target districts increased from 12% to 41%, exceeding the programme's target of 35%.

HealthTech NL B.V., an Amsterdam-based digital health company, deployed an EU-co-funded telemedicine platform across 60 rural clinics in Rwanda and Burundi between 2022 and 2024. The platform was designed with an accessibility-first approach, incorporating audio navigation and simplified interfaces for users with low literacy or visual impairments. Of 38,000 patient consultations completed, 67% were with women and 9% with persons with disabilities. The system integrated maternal care, contraception counselling, and HIV/AIDS prevention modules. Coverage of essential reproductive health services in target districts rose from 34% to 58% over the project period. An accessibility audit confirmed the platform met international standards for assistive technology compatibility.

TransilvaniaCapital S.A., a Bucharest-based investment group, funded a €500,000 Women in Governance programme in partnership with the Romanian Government and UNDP between 2021 and 2023. The programme trained 120 women candidates from rural constituencies for local council elections and provided 30 women with advanced conflict resolution certification recognised by the Romanian Council of Mediation. In the 2020 local elections within target counties, female candidacy rates rose by 14 percentage points and women's seat share on local councils increased from 16% to 23%. Two programme graduates were appointed as official mediators in post-election dispute resolution processes, the first female mediator appointments in those jurisdictions.

SaúdeTech Portugal Lda, a Lisbon-based health technology company, launched a Digital Inclusion for Women programme in 2022 targeting rural communities in Alentejo and Trás-os-Montes. The programme trained 600 women in digital skills, internet safety, and online health service access through 18 community health centres. A fast-track ICT apprenticeship for 45 women from target communities resulted in 32 permanent employment contracts. A subsidised mobile internet access scheme was provided to 200 low-income women for 12 months. By programme end, internet usage among participating women rose from 28% to 79%, and the gender gap in ICT employment within target municipalities narrowed by 11 percentage points.

Hellenic Education Agency, a public body in Athens overseeing 42 higher education institutions, adopted a gender parity action plan in 2022 committing to 50/50 gender balance in all senior and middle management positions within the Agency itself by end of 2024. All appointment panels were required to include at least one gender equality officer, and gender balance criteria were formally incorporated into annual performance review scorecards for all heads of department. By December 2023, women held 49% of senior management positions, up from 33% in 2021. Appointment decision minutes documenting gender balance considerations were made publicly accessible. The Agency also required all 42 supervised institutions to publish gender composition data for their governing bodies.

organisation: French Ministry of Europe and Foreign Affairs
premise: The French Ministry of Europe and Foreign Affairs published an annual contribution to the EU's GAP III monitoring framework each year from 2022 to 2024, covering French bilateral aid activities across 22 partner countries. Each contribution included quantitative data on gender-tagged expenditure, a narrative assessment of progress against GAP III thematic priorities, and case studies of effective practice. France commissioned an independent mid-term evaluation of its GAP III contribution in 2023, conducted by an external consultancy covering the coherence and results of gender-focused bilateral programmes. The evaluation, published publicly in March 2024, found that 68% of French bilateral programmes were gender-responsive and recommended increased investment in reproductive rights dialogue.

EqualAid Belgium asbl, a Brussels-based international development NGO, facilitated a structured civil society dialogue in 2021 to support the European Parliament's deliberations on endorsing GAP III. The organisation convened four thematic hearings attended by 68 MEPs and 40 women's rights organisations from EU partner countries, producing 22 consolidated civil society recommendations on GAP III implementation priorities. EqualAid published a policy brief comparing GAP III commitments with GAP II and identifying six areas requiring stronger oversight. The European Parliament adopted a resolution endorsing GAP III in March 2021, incorporating 14 of the 22 civil society recommendations. EqualAid subsequently monitored and reported annually on Parliament's follow-up to the resolution.

Fabbrica Ferretti S.p.A., a Brescia-based manufacturing company with 480 employees, implemented a Domestic Violence Workplace Policy in January 2023. The policy provided employees experiencing domestic violence with up to 30 days of paid safety leave, flexible scheduling for court or support appointments, and access to a confidential welfare referral service. A partnership with Centro Antiviolenza Brescia provided counselling and shelter referrals for six employees in 2023. Fabbrica Ferretti trained 35 line managers and HR staff to identify signs of domestic violence and refer employees without requiring disclosure. By year-end, 94% of trained managers reported increased confidence in supporting at-risk employees, and zero cases were handled punitively.

BerlinMedienGruppe GmbH, a Berlin-based media organisation, issued an editorial policy in 2022 requiring all conflict and peacetime reporting to apply a unified Istanbul Convention framework when covering gender-based violence. Correspondents were trained in trauma-informed interviewing for GBV survivors, with 62 journalists completing a certified 16-hour module. In 2023, BerlinMedienGruppe published 38 in-depth investigations on gender-based violence, 14 of which covered conflict-affected settings. An independent editorial review confirmed that all 38 investigations complied with the Convention reporting framework, including anonymisation protocols and survivor-centred framing, regardless of whether the setting was peacetime or active conflict.

MadridHoteles S.A., a hospitality group operating 14 hotels across Madrid and Seville, Spain, adopted a Zero Tolerance Against Violence policy in 2022. All 1,200 employees completed a four-hour awareness programme on recognising and responding to violence, harassment, and coercion in both public and private contexts. The company installed discreet emergency alert systems in all 14 hotel lobbies and trained 340 front-of-house staff to activate confidential guest protection procedures. In 2023, 16 incidents involving potential victims of domestic violence were identified and managed, with all 16 individuals connected to police or support services. Employee satisfaction with workplace safety rose from 71% to 89% in the annual survey.

WarszawaLogistyk sp. z o.o., a Warsaw-based logistics company, developed a due diligence protocol in 2022 for handling gender-based violence incidents involving employees or subcontractors, in cooperation with the Polish Ombudsman's Office. The protocol established mandatory internal investigations to be completed within 14 days of any reported incident, with referral to the National Prosecution Service where criminal conduct was identified. A victim compensation fund of PLN 200,000 was set up, from which four employees received compensation in 2023 independent of criminal proceedings. All investigation records were maintained under strict data protection standards and shared with prosecution authorities upon request, resulting in three successful prosecutions within 18 months of protocol implementation.

CeltaTech Ltd, a Dublin-based technology company, partnered with the Irish Department of Justice in 2022 to pilot a national workplace GBV coordination framework under Ireland's Second National Strategy on Domestic, Sexual and Gender-Based Violence. CeltaTech seconded a senior HR specialist to the national implementation working group for 12 months and contributed to standardised victim-centred support pathways for the Irish private sector. Internally, the company launched a cross-functional GBV response team combining HR, legal, occupational health, and security functions, ensuring coordinated responses to all reported incidents. In 2023, CeltaTech received the Excellence in Workplace Safety Award from Safe Ireland for its contribution to national policy and its internal zero-tolerance culture.

StockholmsBank AB, a Stockholm-listed financial institution, launched a Community Anti-Violence Grant Programme in 2022 through its corporate foundation, disbursing SEK 5.4 million to 22 Swedish NGOs working on domestic and sexual violence prevention and victim support. Grant recipients included women's shelters, legal aid clinics, prevention education organisations, and digital safety services. The bank required no exclusivity or branding from recipients and published the full list of grantees and award amounts on its public website. An independent evaluation in 2023 confirmed that the 22 organisations collectively served 3,800 survivors of gender-based violence during the grant period, with 91% reporting improved access to legal and housing support.

GezondheidEdu B.V., a Rotterdam-based health education company, partnered with the Dutch Ministry of Education and 60 primary and secondary schools across North Holland in 2022 to develop and deliver a gender equality curriculum package. The package included age-appropriate classroom modules on gender equality, non-stereotyped role models, healthy relationship skills, and the right to bodily autonomy. Teachers received 12 hours of professional development before delivery. By June 2023, 12,400 students had completed at least four curriculum sessions. A pre-post assessment showed a 24 percentage point increase in students correctly identifying coercive behaviour in relationship scenarios and a 19 percentage point increase in reported confidence to seek help in situations of harm.

EduReform România asbl, a Bucharest-based educational charity, partnered with Romania's National Agency for Equal Opportunities in 2022 to launch a perpetrator intervention programme in three counties. The 26-week programme addressed cognitive distortions, anger regulation, and healthy relationship skills, certified in accordance with Istanbul Convention standards. Forty-eight men completed the programme in 2023, of whom 38 were court-referred and 10 self-referred. A six-month follow-up using police records and victim-reported contact data found recidivism among programme completers at 14%, compared to 38% in a matched comparison group. The National Agency confirmed funding for two additional regional programme sites for 2024 based on the evaluation findings.

The Câmara Municipal de Coimbra, Portugal, established a Multi-Agency Safeguarding Hub in 2022, bringing together the municipal police, social services, health authority, public prosecution, and the local women's shelter in a co-located coordination unit. The Hub operated a shared case management system under a data-sharing protocol approved by the Portuguese Data Protection Authority. In its first year, the Hub processed 312 domestic violence cases, of which 94% received a coordinated multi-agency response within 48 hours. Victim satisfaction surveys completed by 180 survivors rated the coordination as helpful or very helpful in 86% of cases. The model was endorsed by the Portuguese Commission for Citizenship and Gender Equality as a national replication framework.

DikiosyniHellas e.V., an Athens-based legal rights NGO, launched a digital information platform in 2022 providing women victims of violence in Greece with plain-language guides to available legal remedies, including national courts, the European Court of Human Rights, the CEDAW Committee, and the Council of Europe Commissioner for Human Rights. The platform offered content in Greek, English, Albanian, and Arabic, covering petition procedures, admissibility criteria, and submission templates. A helpline staffed by trained legal volunteers provided individual guidance to 420 callers in its first year. The platform was visited by 28,000 unique users in 2023, and DikiosyniHellas filed 14 applications to international bodies on behalf of clients who had exhausted domestic remedies.

ManufactureVerte S.A.S., a Lyons-based composite materials manufacturer, partnered with the Rhône-Alpes child protection services agency in 2022 to integrate child witness support into the domestic violence response offered through its employee assistance scheme. All employees seeking domestic violence protection were automatically assessed for dependent children in the household, with referrals to age-appropriate psychosocial support made within five working days. In 2023, 11 children of affected employees were referred to specialised therapeutic services, including three receiving trauma-focused cognitive behavioural therapy. ManufactureVerte reported a 100% referral completion rate and zero cases in which child witness needs were overlooked in the adult victim support process.

MédiasBelgique SA, a Brussels-based media group, launched a public awareness campaign in 2022 in partnership with the Belgian Institute for the Equality of Women and Men, encouraging bystander reporting of gender-based violence. The campaign reached an estimated audience of 2.1 million across five television channels and four regional newspapers, directing viewers to a single non-emergency reporting line. A companion documentary series profiled eight witnesses who had reported violence and the outcomes achieved. In the 12 months following launch, calls to the reporting line increased by 43%, and the proportion of GBV reports originating from witnesses rather than victims rose from 9% to 19% nationally.

RisortoItalia S.r.l., a Naples-based hotel and catering group, established an internal victim support fund in 2022 through which employees who were victims of gender-based violence could access emergency financial assistance while awaiting compensation from perpetrators or state victim support funds. The fund, capitalised at €150,000 and replenished annually, disbursed payments to seven employees in its first year, averaging €4,200 per beneficiary. The company's legal team provided free representation in compensation claims before Italian civil courts, resulting in five successful judgments ordering perpetrator payment. In the two cases where perpetrators were unable to pay, employees were assisted in applying to Italy's state victim compensation fund under Legislative Decree 209/2005.

RheinFracht GmbH, a Frankfurt-based freight logistics company, updated its disciplinary code in 2022 following Germany's implementation of the psychological violence provisions of the Istanbul Convention. The code explicitly defined psychologically coercive conduct — including sustained threats, intimidation, and systematic humiliation — as a dismissible offence and obligated the company to refer credible cases to the public prosecutor. Training on recognising and reporting psychological violence was delivered to 280 supervisors and team leaders. In 2023, the company investigated four complaints of coercive conduct, dismissed two employees, issued formal warnings in the remaining cases, and referred all four to the Frankfurt Prosecution Service. The updated code was endorsed by the works council as compliant with the German Act on Violence Against Women.

TecnoBarcelona S.L., a Barcelona-based software development firm, updated its code of conduct and digital security policy in 2022 following Spain's reform of the Criminal Code to include stalking as a specific offence under Article 172 ter, aligned with Istanbul Convention requirements. The company established a digital harassment monitoring protocol for company-issued devices and platforms, training cybersecurity and HR teams to identify patterns of repeated threatening or surveillance behaviour. In 2023, TecnoBarcelona identified and investigated two cases of stalking involving employees, referred both to the National Police's cybercrime unit, and supported affected employees through the criminal complaint process. Both cases resulted in restraining orders being issued within three months of the initial report.

FinPolska S.A., a Warsaw-based financial services group, incorporated FGM prevention into its international supply chain ethics programme in 2022, operating across partner organisations in six African countries. The programme required all partner organisations to certify that no employees or community members in their sphere of influence were subjected to or encouraged to undergo FGM, and to participate in awareness training. FinPolska funded a legal literacy programme delivered by a Nairobi-based NGO to 900 women and girls in Kenya and Tanzania, covering their rights under national laws criminalising FGM. By 2023, 14 FGM prevention cases had been reported to local authorities with support from the programme's legal helpline.

HealthCare Ireland Group plc, a Dublin-based healthcare provider, submitted data to a Department of Justice public consultation in 2022 documenting the deterrent effect of clear, escalating disciplinary sanctions on workplace gender-based harassment. The submission showed that explicit, proportionate sanctions had reduced repeat harassment incidents within the organisation from 12 per year in 2016 to 2 per year in 2021. HealthCare Ireland updated its professional standards charter to align offence categorisation and sanctions with Istanbul Convention Article 45, providing responses calibrated to severity. The charter's most serious offences triggered permanent professional debarment and mandatory referral to the Health and Social Care Regulatory Forum, ensuring effective and dissuasive consequences.

Göteborgs Utbildningsförvaltning, the education authority of Gothenburg, Sweden, implemented a safeguarding protocol in 2022 enabling school principals to activate local police emergency barring orders for employees identified as victims of domestic violence facing imminent danger at or near school premises. The protocol was developed in partnership with the Gothenburg Police District and the city's social services authority and provided for barring orders to be issued within four hours of a risk assessment triggering the emergency threshold. In 2023, the protocol was activated in three cases; all three resulted in barring orders prohibiting perpetrators from approaching the victim's workplace and residence, with orders issued within an average of 2.8 hours. All three employees continued working without interruption and received ongoing occupational health support.

Rijksdienst Jeugdzorg, a Dutch public authority for youth welfare, submitted a formal policy recommendation to the Dutch Ministry of Justice in 2022 supporting the extension of the statute of limitations for serious sexual offences against minors, based on an analysis of 84 closed cases in which victims had been unable to initiate prosecution due to limitation periods expiring before they were psychologically ready to report. The recommendation was adopted in the 2023 Criminal Procedure Reform Act, which extended the limitation period for sexual offences against minors from 12 to 20 years post-majority, in line with Istanbul Convention requirements. Rijksdienst Jeugdzorg published a guidance note for youth welfare workers explaining the new provisions and referral rights for victims of historical sexual abuse.

AnasaziRomania asbl, a Bucharest-based cross-border victim support NGO operating across Romania, Moldova, and Ukraine, established a transnational information-sharing protocol in 2022 modelled on the mutual legal assistance principles of the Istanbul Convention. Under the protocol, when a victim relocated across a border, the sending country organisation notified the receiving counterpart within 24 hours, transmitting anonymised risk assessments and referral documentation. Where intelligence suggested imminent risk of further violence, information was transmitted proactively without a prior request. In 2023, 47 cross-border referrals were completed, with 38 resulting in the receiving organisation establishing protective contact within 48 hours. The protocol was formally recognised by the Romanian Ministry of Justice as a model for inter-state victim protection coordination.
